item.title: Return of the Meme: 1091 (thinkin' about Rome I)
item.description:
Good morning, let's have the first of four themed posts about Roman history because, why not? This first post goes from Romulus and Remus to Julius Caesar. Let's get that bread.
Rome neutralized the Carthaginian experience on sea for a few battles in the First Punic War with these bridges that basically turned land battles into sea battles. They were known as the Corvus. Unfortunately, they also made these ships top-heavy and easier to sink during storms, so eventually, the Romans stopped using them.
Romans loved these things. You might say they were... Fascinated... by them. (The joke is that these were known as fascinum.)
Not sure if this bust is Scipio Africanus, or Marcus Regulus. Both were governors of Sicily and invaded Africa. The latter got his ass handed to him, though.
Battle of Cynocephale, I believe. Or was it Pydna? Fuck. I can't remember.
As we'll see later, he threw them into the sea and attacked anyway... and lost.
The Romans basically just... kept coming.
Shipwrecked Carthaginian Quinqueremes washing up on Italian shores basically allowed the romans to build exact copies of their ships... and helped neutralize Carthaginian naval advantage.
Publius Cornelius Pulcher.
THIS is the battle of Cynocephale, for sure.
Mithradates VI was a fascinating dude.
Actually, the romans never salted the farmland around Carthage, its a historical myth.
A wojak version of the Surrender of Vercingetorix, ending the Gallic Wars.
Yeaaah.
The invention of the Manipolar legion is one reason for Rome's success.
Marc Antony won.
Wheeee
The difference in ideology of the soldiers between the republic and the later empire can be stark..
Claudius Pulcher
Spoiler alert: no.
Pippin tax Today's message from Pippin: "Beep. I love you "
item.dc:creator: CodyBurkett

item.title: Angry workplace rant
item.description: Don't really care if this dies in user sub I just need to scream into the void. Can't just quit I really need to have some form of income. Already applied to three other places including a library and a local dispensary. None of them are hiring for that much more an hour but at least I will be more respected.

item.title: Penn Jillette on not being a libertarian any more
item.description: Seems reasonable to me. Other interesting bits in the article too, including his very low opinion of Trump. https://www.cracked.com/amp/article_40871_penn-jillette-wants-to-talk-it-all-out.html FP edit: thanks! And I should've said that I think this is great of Jillette. Tons of people don't change their minds, especially about something they've said publicly. Those that do often claim they haven't changed, or don't publicly admit their mistake, or waste so much time explaining why their mistake was justified that they're practically still arguing for their mistaken position. So I applaud him saying "I need to be very ashamed of [my old position]," and I hope I can manage the same with my own errors.
item.dc:creator: wingineer

item.title: Orc songs
item.description:
This is a lore account on cultural Orcs, inspired by this catchy tune from The Return of the King (1980).
The song "Where There's a Whip, There's a Way" in the animated TV-film by Rankin/Bass. It is performed by Glenn Yarbrough (1930 - 2016, seen above), with music by Maury Laws (1923 - 2019), and lyrics by Jules Bass (1935 - 2022) and/or Yarbrough; it is thus not written by Tolkien but it is inspired by lore.
At the end of the Third Age came Frodo Baggins and Samwise Gamgee into Mordor disguised as Orcs. Soon a company of Orcs marched by them, and the pair tried to hide in the shadow of a cliff on the side of the road (The Return of the King; VI; II, onwards);
"The leading orcs came loping along, panting, holding their heads down. They were a gang of the smaller breeds being driven unwilling to their Dark Lord’s wars; all they cared for was to get the march over and escape the whip. Beside them, running up and down the line, went two of the large fierce uruks, cracking lashes and shouting."
Unforturnatley the hobbits were discovered, mistaken for deserters and forced to join the painful march: "Now and again the orc-driver fell back and jeered at them. `There now!’ he laughed, flicking at their legs. `Where there’s a whip there’s a will, my slugs. Hold up! I’d give you a nice freshener now, only you’ll get as much lash as your skins will carry when you come in late to your camp. Do you good. Don’t you know we’re at war?’"
It is rare to read about Orcs singing, perhaps because Tolkien did not want to imagine their voices which were "as the clashing of stones" (The History of Middle-earth 10; I; §127). He removed or made references vaguer over time. One example of the latter is the duel between Morgoth, the First Age's Dark Lord, and Fingolfin, King of the High Elves, that ended with Morgoth painfully wounded and Fingolfin slain. In ca 1930 is told "The Orcs sing of that duel at the gates, but the Elves do not" (HoME 4; III. IX), but by 1937-1938 it became "The Orcs make no boast of that duel at the gate; neither do the Elves sing of it, for sorrow;" (HoME 5; VI; XI); and it remained 'boast' into The Silmarillion (Silm; III; XVIII, art by Ryofy).
Boast is implied to be or include song, and there are other indirect examples in the First Age. Galadriel's brother Finrod was out in the wild when he first met humans (Silmarillion; III; XVII, art by Elena Kukanova): "...[Finrod] saw lights in the evening, and far off he heard the sound of song.... At first he feared that a raid of Orcs had passed the leaguer of the North, but as he drew near he perceived that it was not so; for the singers used a tongue that he had not heard before, neither that of Dwarves nor of Orcs."
The best cases are however found in The Hobbit, for they have lyrics. To avoid confusion shall first be noted that despite adaptations, and though it is otherwise in other fantasy settings, in Middle-earth are Orc and Orch simply synonyms with Goblin. They are not different creatures (The Hobbit; Foreword): "Orc is not an English word. It occurs in one or two places but is usually translated goblin..." The first song is "Clap! Snap! The black crack!" [so called after its first line]. It is sung, or croaked, when Bilbo and the Dwarves are captured by Goblins in the Misty Mountains and taken to Goblin Town;
"Clap! Snap! the black crack! Grip, grab! Pinch, nab! And down down to Goblin-town You go, my lad! Clash, crash! Crush, smash! Hammer and tongs! Knocker and gongs! Pound, pound, far underground! Ho, ho! my lad! Swish, smack! Whip crack! Batter and beat! Yammer and bleat! Work, work! Nor dare to shirk, While Goblins quaff, and Goblins laugh, Round and round far underground Below, my lad!” – TH; IV (art by John Howe)
It is adapted as "Down, Down, To Goblin Town" in the animated The Hobbit (1977) by Rankin/Bass. Performed by Thurl Ravenscroft (1914 - 2005), with music by Laws and lyrics by Bass, this edition cuts most references to the tools [for torture] in favour of references to the dark place they were brought to. The second song is sung after Gandalf, Bilbo and the Dwarves fled the mountain. Hunted by Wargs and Goblins they climbed up fir trees. There the Goblins mocked them, singing [here give uninterrupted];
"Fifteen birds in five firtrees, their feathers were fanned in a fiery breeze! But, funny little birds, they had no wings! O what shall we do with the funny little things? Roast 'em alive, or stew them in a pot; fry them, boil them and eat them hot?" "Burn, burn tree and fern! Shrivel and scorch! A fizzling torch To light the night for our delight, Ya hey! Bake and toast 'em, fry and roast ’em till beards blaze, and eyes glaze; till hair smells and skins crack, fat melts, and bones black in cinders lie beneath the sky! So dwarves shall die, and light the night for our delight, Ya hey! Ya-harri-hey! Ya hoy!" – TH; VI (art by Alan Lee)
In the animated film this is adapted by Laws (music) and Bass (lyrics) as "Funny Little Things", performed by Ravenscroft. The text is close to the source, and mainly have some lines moved around or repeated. Finally, though "Where There's a Whip, There's a Way" is not in The Lord of the Rings we do still read about singing in The Two Towers. When the army of Isengard, that consisted of a mix of Men, Orcs and half-breeds as Uruks, drew near to the Hornburg the defenders "could hear, borne over the dark, the sound of harsh singing" (TTT; Helm's Deep).
And on the border to Mordor was Frodo captured by the Orcs and Uruks, led by the two captains Gorbag and Shagrat. Samwise followed them close to the tower of Cirith Ungol: "Already the Orcs with their burden had passed inside. Gorbag and Shagrat were drawing near the gate. Sam heard a burst of hoarse singing, blaring of horns and banging of gongs, a hideous clamour." – TTT; IV; X (art by Alan Lee)
I wish you a fine Friday, and end with that if you wish to be notified and not miss when I post Tolkien lore, art, memes and other related content I am glad to be of service. Comment "Where there's a whip" or "I say whip it" for me to welcome you to our fellowship.
For more information on Orcs, Goblins, Uruk-Hai and other names https://imgur.com/gallery/MfttGFV — — — — — For more of my Tolkien lore posts, enjoy these two indexes: Character bios https://imgur.com/gallery/axV2mPF Folks, animals, items, etc https://imgur.com/gallery/AQK9pJL
item.dc:creator: Ngugi

item.dc:creator: dickwitfo

item.title: OC webcomic 6 - Broken Looking Glass
item.description: Buddy is, to put it gently, a ginormous coward. He doesn't even play with a teddy if it happens to be larger than his own head, and then only if it's facing directly away from him. Like in the comic, if I were to tap on the floorboards, once he realizes that it's not a toy, he must assume it's some horrible monster by the way he sprints away. Buddy doesn't realize it's MY hand, because my arm is slightly obscured by a blanket. After regaining his composure, he does sometimes actually charge at the tapping fingers, usually just running by with a light tap. Expect more comic strips soon-ish ^^ -Vague
item.dc:creator: BrokenLookingGlassComics

item.title: The level of detail is incredible!
item.dc:creator: lifonaut

item.title: An Atlas a day.
item.description: Impromptu heel walk session. It was rainy and cold and he decided it was better to stay close to me and try to stay under the umbrella. He kept himself in this walk position almost the whole time and chose to maintain eye contact for a good bit of it. Though he may have been driven by the cold rain to do this, he still made the choice and I see it as a win.
item.dc:creator: AtlasHuggedrescuepup

item.title: Interview with the Vampire - Louis
item.description: I did an interview with a journalist for a newspaper article on me so I showed up as Louie from Interview with the Vampire. The journalist was thrilled. ?
item.dc:creator: knightmage15

item.title: Farewell my boy. So glad I saved you from Parvo, 9 years is a lot more than the 2 weeks the vet originally thought! We will never forget you, thank you for everything you gave me!
item.description:
Drying off after the last swim with dad the day we got the prognosis
Moved a bed into the living room for the last 2 weeks once we got the news.
The Bills lost, but we still had a good time.
Our adventure buddies (yes there is a second dog in this picture…)
Don’t forget to be lazy.
We took our dogs to the Tetons for our honeymoon and this was our campsite.
item.dc:creator: spencedt

item.title: The Walk Home - an acrylic painting of mine.
item.description: did this one for my mom for christmas. My site where i post all my art is http://www.flookart.com for anybody interested #rain #creativity #mildly_interesting #painting #nostalgia
item.dc:creator: FLOOKO
